core_rule: French nouns carry gender and number; determiners and many adjectives must match the noun they belong to.
when_to_use: Use this whenever a noun phrase contains an article, adjective, or pronoun that must agree with the noun.
contrast_rules: English does not mark gender on articles; French does. | Plural -s is often silent but still controls written agreement.
main_traps: guessing gender from English | forgetting plural marks in writing | agreeing with the nearest word instead of the noun
anchor_examples: un livre blanc | une table blanche | des livres blancs
xray_sentence: Une petite table blanche est dans la cuisine.
xray_marks: Une table=>controller=>the feminine singular noun controls the phrase||petite...blanche=>agreement_marks=>both adjectives take feminine singular endings

name_en: Definite articles: le, la, l', les
name_de: Bestimmte Artikel: le, la, l', les
core_rule: Use le, la, l', or les when the noun is specific, known, or treated as a general category.
when_to_use: Use this when English uses “the,” or when French names a general class like languages, sports, preferences, and abstract nouns.
contrast_rules: Le/la/les can mean “the” or a whole category; un/une introduces one item. | L' is only for singular nouns before a vowel or silent h.
main_traps: using le/la from sound only | forgetting l' before a vowel | using des for a general category
anchor_examples: le livre | la ville | l'école | les enfants
xray_sentence: J'aime la musique et les films français.
xray_marks: la musique=>general_category=>a whole category uses a definite article||les films=>plural_known_category=>plural category also keeps the definite article

core_rule: Use un, une, or des to introduce an unspecified person or thing.
when_to_use: Use this when the listener does not yet know which item you mean, or when English uses “a,” “an,” or “some.”
contrast_rules: Indefinite articles introduce; definite articles identify. | Des is plural “some,” not a definite plural.
main_traps: using le/la for a new item | forgetting des in plural | using une with masculine nouns
anchor_examples: J'ai un frère. | Elle cherche une adresse. | Nous avons des questions.
xray_sentence: J'ai une question et des idées.
xray_marks: une question=>new_singular=>an unspecified singular item is introduced||des idées=>new_plural=>plural new items use des

name_en: Partitive articles: du, de la, de l'
name_de: Teilungsartikel: du, de la, de l'
core_rule: Use partitive articles for an unspecified amount of something not counted as individual units.
when_to_use: Use this with food, drink, materials, and abstract quantities when you mean “some” or “any amount of.”
contrast_rules: Partitive du/de la is quantity; definite le/la is the whole category. | After a negative quantity, the article often changes to de.
main_traps: using le/la when you mean some | using des with mass nouns | forgetting de l' before vowels
anchor_examples: Je bois du café. | Elle mange de la soupe. | Il a de l'énergie.
xray_sentence: Je voudrais du pain et de l'eau.
xray_marks: du pain=>partitive_quantity=>an unspecified amount of bread||de l'eau=>elided_partitive=>de l' appears before a vowel

core_rule: French possessives agree with the thing possessed, not with the owner.
when_to_use: Use this before a noun when you want to say my, your, his, her, our, or their.
contrast_rules: English possessives agree with the owner; French possessives agree with the noun. | Mon is used before feminine vowel-initial nouns.
main_traps: using ma before a vowel | agreeing with the owner | forgetting plural mes
anchor_examples: mon livre | ma voiture | mon amie | mes clés
xray_sentence: Mon amie arrive avec mes clés.
xray_marks: Mon amie=>vowel_exception=>mon appears before feminine amie because it starts with a vowel||mes clés=>plural_possessed=>the possessed noun is plural, so mes is required

core_rule: Use ce, cet, cette, or ces to point to a noun as “this” or “that.”
when_to_use: Use this when identifying, pointing out, or contrasting a specific noun in the situation.
contrast_rules: Cet is masculine singular before a vowel sound; cette is feminine. | French does not distinguish this/that in the determiner itself.
main_traps: using ce before a vowel | mixing cette and cet | forgetting plural ces
anchor_examples: ce livre | cet homme | cette idée | ces questions
xray_sentence: Cet hôtel est près de cette gare.
xray_marks: Cet hôtel=>masculine_vowel=>cet replaces ce before a vowel sound||cette gare=>feminine_singular=>feminine singular uses cette

core_rule: Subject pronouns name who does the action; on often means we, people, or someone depending on context.
when_to_use: Use this whenever the verb needs an explicit subject, especially to distinguish il/elle, ils/elles, nous, vous, and on.
contrast_rules: On is grammatically singular even when it means “we.” | Vous can be plural or formal singular.
main_traps: using nous endings after on | mixing ils and elles | using tu in formal contexts
anchor_examples: On va au marché avec nos amis.
exercise_tags: On=>singular_form=>on uses third-person singular verb form||nos amis=>we_meaning=>the context can still mean “we”
xray_sentence: cefr | delf_a1 | kwiziq_a1

core_rule: Use the present tense for current actions, habits, general facts, and near-future context when time is clear.
when_to_use: Use this as the base tense for everyday statements and questions before adding past or future frames.
contrast_rules: The French present can cover English “do,” “am doing,” and sometimes scheduled near future. | Irregular high-frequency verbs must be learned as forms, not rules.
main_traps: using infinitive after a subject | mixing nous/vous endings | treating être/avoir/aller/faire as regular
anchor_examples: je parle | nous finissons | ils prennent | elle va
xray_sentence: Nous faisons nos devoirs et elle prend le train.
xray_marks: Nous faisons=>irregular_present=>faire has an irregular nous form||elle prend=>third_person=>prendre changes stem in the singular

core_rule: Place ne before the conjugated verb and pas after it to make a standard negative sentence.
when_to_use: Use this for simple negative statements in written or careful French.
contrast_rules: Spoken French often drops ne; the notebook keeps the full written frame. | Article changes after negation are a separate A2 rule.
main_traps: forgetting ne in writing | placing pas before the verb | confusing n' before a vowel
anchor_examples: Je ne comprends pas. | Elle n'aime pas ça. | Nous ne partons pas.
xray_sentence: Elle n'aime pas le café.
xray_marks: n'aime=>elided_ne=>ne becomes n' before a vowel||pas=>negative_marker=>pas closes the negation frame after the verb

core_rule: French questions can use rising intonation, est-ce que, or inversion depending on formality.
when_to_use: Use this when asking for information while keeping the right register.
pattern: intonation / est-ce que + statement order / inversion
contrast_rules: Est-ce que keeps normal word order; inversion changes subject-verb order. | Question words can stand at the start or stay in spoken position.
main_traps: using inversion and est-ce que together | forgetting hyphen in inversion | using English word order after question words
anchor_examples: Tu viens ? | Est-ce que tu viens ? | Où habites-tu ?
xray_sentence: Est-ce que tu viens demain ?
xray_marks: Est-ce que=>question_frame=>keeps normal statement order after it||tu viens=>statement_order=>subject and verb stay in their regular order

core_rule: Most adjectives agree with the noun in gender and number, and many common adjectives come after the noun.
when_to_use: Use this whenever a noun is described by color, quality, nationality, size, or evaluation.
pattern: noun + adjective agreement; some short adjectives before noun
contrast_rules: Position can change meaning for some adjectives. | Agreement is written even when final letters are silent.
main_traps: leaving adjective masculine singular | placing every adjective after the noun | forgetting plural -s
anchor_examples: une maison blanche | de petits cafés | un ancien professeur
xray_sentence: De petites maisons blanches bordent la rue.
xray_marks: petites=>before_noun_agreement=>the adjective before the noun still agrees feminine plural||blanches=>after_noun_agreement=>the color adjective after the noun also agrees

core_rule: Use aller in the present plus an infinitive to talk about an upcoming action.
when_to_use: Use this for plans, intentions, and events felt close or already arranged.
contrast_rules: Futur proche is more immediate or planned than future simple. | Only aller is conjugated; the second verb stays infinitive.
main_traps: conjugating both verbs | using être instead of aller | using future simple for a near plan
anchor_examples: Je vais partir. | Nous allons étudier. | Ils vont arriver.
xray_sentence: Nous allons visiter Paris demain.
xray_marks: allons=>conjugated_aller=>only aller changes for the subject||visiter=>infinitive=>the action verb stays infinitive

core_rule: Use le/la/les for direct objects and lui/leur for indirect objects introduced by à.
when_to_use: Use this when replacing a known person or thing so the sentence is less repetitive.
pattern: pronoun before conjugated verb: le/la/les or lui/leur
contrast_rules: Direct object pronouns answer whom/what; indirect pronouns usually answer to whom. | Lui/leur do not show gender.
main_traps: using lui for a direct object | placing pronouns after the verb | using le for à + person
anchor_examples: Je la vois. | Je lui parle. | Nous les invitons.
xray_sentence: Je lui parle, mais je la vois rarement.
xray_marks: lui=>indirect_person=>parler à someone becomes lui||la=>direct_object=>voir someone directly becomes la

core_rule: Use y for à/place ideas and en for de/quantity ideas.
when_to_use: Use this when the replaced phrase is not a simple direct object.
pattern: y = à/place phrase; en = de/quantity phrase
contrast_rules: Y often points to a place or à + thing; en points to de + noun or a quantity. | People after à usually use lui/leur, not y.
main_traps: using en for direct objects | using y for people after à | forgetting quantity with en
anchor_examples: J'y vais. | J'en veux deux. | Elle en parle.
xray_sentence: Je vais au marché et j'en achète deux.
xray_marks: au marché=>place_source=>the place could be replaced by y||en=>quantity_pronoun=>en points back to a de/quantity idea

core_rule: After a negative quantity, change du, de la, de l', des, un, or une to de or d'.
when_to_use: Use this when a negative sentence says there is no amount or no example of something.
contrast_rules: Ce n'est pas keeps the original article because être identifies rather than counts. | Definite articles usually stay definite after negation.
main_traps: keeping du/des after pas | changing articles after être | forgetting d' before a vowel
anchor_examples: Je ne bois pas de café. | Ce n'est pas du café. | Elle n'a pas d'argent.
xray_sentence: Je ne bois pas de café.
xray_marks: ne...pas=>negative_frame=>the verb is negated by the full frame||de café=>negative_quantity=>du café becomes de café after pas

core_rule: Use avoir in the present plus the past participle to report a completed past action.
when_to_use: Use this for events that happened and are treated as finished.
contrast_rules: Passé composé presents a completed event; imparfait presents background or habit. | Most verbs use avoir, but movement/reflexive verbs often do not.
main_traps: using the infinitive instead of participle | forgetting avoir | using imparfait for completed events
anchor_examples: J'ai mangé. | Nous avons fini. | Elle a vendu sa voiture.
xray_sentence: Hier, nous avons fini le projet.
xray_marks: avons=>auxiliary_avoir=>avoir carries person and number||fini=>past_participle=>the completed action sits in the participle

core_rule: Use être with movement/reflexive verbs in passé composé, and make the participle agree with the subject.
when_to_use: Use this for the common être verbs like aller, venir, entrer, sortir, naître, mourir, and reflexive verbs.
contrast_rules: Être verbs show subject agreement; avoir verbs usually do not. | Some movement verbs switch auxiliary when they take a direct object.
main_traps: using avoir with aller/venir | forgetting agreement | adding agreement to every avoir verb
anchor_examples: Elle est arrivée. | Ils sont sortis. | Nous nous sommes levés.
xray_sentence: Les étudiantes sont arrivées tôt.
xray_marks: sont=>auxiliary_etre=>être is the auxiliary for arriver||arrivées=>subject_agreement=>the participle agrees feminine plural

core_rule: Reflexive verbs use a pronoun that points back to the subject.
when_to_use: Use this for daily routines, reciprocal actions, and verbs whose French form is reflexive.
contrast_rules: Reflexive meaning is not always visible in English. | In passé composé, reflexives normally use être.
main_traps: forgetting the reflexive pronoun | using the wrong pronoun | treating every reflexive as literal self-action
anchor_examples: je me lève | nous nous parlons | elle s'est habillée
xray_sentence: Nous nous levons à sept heures.
xray_marks: Nous=>subject=>the subject controls the reflexive pronoun||nous levons=>reflexive_pair=>the second nous belongs to the verb frame

core_rule: Use imparfait for past background, habits, descriptions, and actions in progress.
when_to_use: Use this when the past action is not framed as a single completed event.
contrast_rules: Imparfait is not simply “was -ing”; it can describe habits and states too. | Passé composé is used for bounded events.
main_traps: using passé composé for every past verb | treating description as an event | forgetting nous-stem exceptions
anchor_examples: Quand j'étais petit, je jouais dehors. | Il faisait froid. | Nous allions souvent au parc.
xray_sentence: Il faisait froid et les rues étaient vides.
xray_marks: faisait=>background_state=>weather is described as a past state||étaient=>description=>the scene is held open, not completed

core_rule: In mixed past narration, use imparfait for the scene and passé composé for the event that happens.
when_to_use: Use this when one past action interrupts, changes, or advances a background situation.
contrast_rules: Imparfait sets the stage; passé composé moves the story. | A long action can still be passé composé if it is treated as bounded.
main_traps: using one past tense for everything | missing interruption cues | choosing tense from verb meaning only
anchor_examples: Je lisais quand le téléphone a sonné. | Il pleuvait, puis le soleil est sorti.
xray_sentence: Je lisais quand le téléphone a sonné.
xray_marks: lisais=>background=>reading was already in progress||a sonné=>event=>the ringing is the bounded event

core_rule: Use plus/aussi/moins ... que for comparison and le/la/les plus ... for the superlative.
when_to_use: Use this to compare qualities, quantities, or actions.
pattern: plus/aussi/moins + adjective/adverb + que; le plus + adjective
contrast_rules: Comparatives compare two things; superlatives pick an extreme inside a group. | Adjectives still agree with the noun.
main_traps: forgetting que | using de instead of que | forgetting adjective agreement
anchor_examples: plus grand que | aussi vite que | la plus intéressante
xray_sentence: Cette solution est plus simple que l'autre.
xray_marks: plus simple=>comparative=>plus marks the higher degree||que=>comparison_link=>que introduces what it is compared with

core_rule: Use qui for the subject role, que for the object role, and où for place or time inside a relative clause.
when_to_use: Use this to combine two sentences around a noun without repeating it.
contrast_rules: Qui/que are chosen by the missing role inside the relative clause, not by the noun's gender. | Où can refer to time as well as place.
main_traps: using qui for every relative clause | choosing by gender | forgetting elision qu'
anchor_examples: C'est le film que j'aime. | La femme qui parle est médecin. | Le jour où tu viens.
xray_sentence: La ville où j'habite est calme.
xray_marks: La ville=>antecedent=>the noun being specified||où j'habite=>place_clause=>où fills the place role inside the clause

core_rule: Use depuis for duration still connected to now, pendant for a bounded duration, and pour for planned duration.
when_to_use: Use this when the time phrase changes whether an action is ongoing, completed, or intended.
pattern: depuis + present/past point; pendant + bounded duration; pour + planned duration
contrast_rules: Depuis often uses present in French where English uses present perfect. | Pendant measures duration without saying it continues now.
main_traps: using pour for past duration | translating “for” mechanically | using passé composé after depuis for ongoing states
anchor_examples: J'habite ici depuis deux ans. | Il a dormi pendant huit heures. | Je pars pour trois jours.
xray_sentence: J'étudie le français depuis deux ans.
xray_marks: J'étudie=>present_ongoing=>French uses present for an action continuing now||depuis deux ans=>duration_to_now=>depuis connects the duration to the present

core_rule: In affirmative commands, object pronouns follow the verb and attach with hyphens.
when_to_use: Use this when giving direct instructions that include le, la, les, moi, nous, lui, leur, y, or en.
pattern: imperative- pronoun(s); negative command keeps normal pronoun order
contrast_rules: Affirmative command order differs from normal pronoun position. | Me and te become moi and toi after affirmative imperatives.
main_traps: putting pronoun before affirmative imperative | forgetting hyphens | using me/te after the verb
anchor_examples: Donne-le-moi. | N'y va pas. | Prends-en deux.
xray_sentence: Donne-le-moi demain.
xray_marks: Donne=>imperative_verb=>the command starts with the verb||le-moi=>attached_pronouns=>pronouns follow and attach in affirmative commands

name_en: Si type 1: present condition, future result
core_rule: In a real si-condition, keep the si-clause in the present and put the consequence in future simple or imperative.
when_to_use: Use this for a possible future condition and its likely result or instruction.
contrast_rules: Do not put future simple immediately after si. | Do not replace the requested si frame with quand.
main_traps: using future after si | using conditional for a real result | replacing si with quand
anchor_examples: Si tu viens, on ira. | Si tu as faim, mange. | S'il pleut, prends ton manteau.
xray_sentence: Si je finis tôt, je t'appellerai.
xray_marks: Si=>condition_trigger=>opens a real future condition||finis=>present_si_clause=>the verb after si stays present||appellerai=>future_result=>the result clause carries the future

name_en: Si type 2: imperfect condition, conditional result
core_rule: Use imparfait after si and conditional present in the result for an unreal or unlikely present/future situation.
when_to_use: Use this for hypotheses, advice, and imagined conditions that are not presented as real facts.
contrast_rules: Si type 1 is real/open; si type 2 is hypothetical. | The conditional belongs in the result, not after si.
main_traps: using conditional after si | using present for an unreal condition | confusing advice with real future
anchor_examples: Si j'avais le temps, je voyagerais. | Si tu voulais, tu pourrais venir.
xray_sentence: Si j'avais le temps, je partirais.
xray_marks: j'avais=>imperfect_condition=>the si-clause uses imparfait for the hypothesis||partirais=>conditional_result=>the result uses conditional present

core_rule: Use future simple for future facts, predictions, promises, and consequences that are not framed as immediate plans.
when_to_use: Use this when the future is stated more formally, remotely, or as a result in a si type 1 frame.
pattern: infinitive stem + future endings; irregular future stems
contrast_rules: Futur proche feels planned or near; future simple can be broader, formal, or consequential. | After si, future simple goes in the result clause only.
main_traps: using futur proche everywhere | putting future after si | forgetting irregular stems
anchor_examples: Je partirai demain. | Nous aurons le temps. | Elle fera attention.
xray_sentence: Demain, nous aurons plus de temps.
xray_marks: aurons=>irregular_future=>avoir uses the future stem aur-||demain=>future_context=>the time phrase anchors the future statement

core_rule: Use conditional present for hypothetical results, polite requests, and future-in-the-past contexts.
when_to_use: Use this when the action depends on a condition, softens a request, or is reported from a past viewpoint.
contrast_rules: Conditional is not the same as future simple; it often marks dependency or politeness. | In si type 2, conditional belongs in the result clause.
main_traps: using conditional for certain future | putting it after si | confusing politeness with tense
anchor_examples: Je voudrais un café. | Elle viendrait si elle pouvait. | Il a dit qu'il partirait.
xray_sentence: Je viendrais si j'avais le temps.
xray_marks: viendrais=>conditional_result=>the result is hypothetical||si j'avais=>condition=>the condition uses imparfait

core_rule: Use imparfait for ongoing background, habit, or description; use passé composé for the bounded event that moves the story forward.
when_to_use: Use this in past narration when a scene and an event appear together.
pattern: imparfait = background/habit; passé composé = completed event
contrast_rules: Imparfait is not a generic past tense. | Passé composé is not only for short actions; it marks a bounded event.
main_traps: using passé composé for the whole past sentence | using imparfait for completed events | ignoring time boundaries
anchor_examples: Il pleuvait quand je suis sorti. | Je lisais quand le téléphone a sonné. | Chaque été, nous allions à Lyon.
xray_sentence: Il pleuvait quand je suis sorti.
xray_marks: pleuvait=>background=>weather was already in progress||suis sorti=>bounded_event=>leaving is the completed event

core_rule: French object pronouns follow a fixed order before the verb or around imperatives.
when_to_use: Use this when two pronouns appear together, especially direct/indirect objects with y or en.
contrast_rules: Pronoun order is structural, not based on English meaning. | Affirmative imperatives use a different post-verbal order.
main_traps: translating English order | mixing lui before le | forgetting y/en positions
anchor_examples: Je le lui donne. | Il nous en parle. | Donne-le-lui.
xray_sentence: Je le lui donne demain.
xray_marks: le=>direct_object_slot=>direct object pronoun comes before lui/leur||lui=>indirect_object_slot=>indirect person pronoun follows le/la/les

core_rule: Choose y for à/place ideas and en for de/quantity ideas, but keep person complements separate.
when_to_use: Use this when a sentence has an abstract phrase, quantity, or place that would be repeated.
pattern: y = à/place/thing; en = de/quantity; lui/leur = à + person
contrast_rules: Y does not replace à + person in standard object-pronoun use. | En can replace a quantity even when the number remains.
main_traps: using y for people | using en for direct objects | dropping the remaining number
anchor_examples: Je pense à Paris -> J'y pense. | Je parle de ce livre -> J'en parle. | J'en veux trois.
xray_sentence: Je pense à ce projet et j'en parle souvent.
xray_marks: à ce projet=>y_candidate=>à + thing can become y with penser||en=>de_link=>parler de becomes en parler

core_rule: Use dont when the missing link inside the relative clause would be de + noun.
when_to_use: Use this after verbs, adjectives, or nouns that require de, such as parler de, avoir besoin de, être fier de.
contrast_rules: Dont is not a universal “whose”; it specifically covers a de-link. | Qui/que are chosen by subject/object role, not de.
main_traps: using que after parler de | using dont without a de-link | translating English whose mechanically
anchor_examples: Le livre dont je parle. | La personne dont j'ai besoin. | Le film dont il est fier.
xray_sentence: C'est le problème dont nous parlons.
xray_marks: le problème=>antecedent=>the noun is the topic being specified||dont=>de_link=>dont replaces de ce problème inside parlons de

core_rule: Reported speech keeps the meaning of the original words while embedding them under a reporting verb.
when_to_use: Use this when saying what someone says, thinks, asks, or announces.
contrast_rules: Reported questions do not keep direct question word order. | Tense shifts become more important at higher levels.
main_traps: keeping direct question order | forgetting que/si | changing pronouns without checking viewpoint
anchor_examples: Il dit qu'il vient. | Elle demande si tu comprends. | Il pense que c'est vrai.
xray_sentence: Elle demande si tu viens demain.
xray_marks: demande=>reporting_verb=>opens indirect speech||si tu viens=>embedded_question=>yes/no question becomes si + statement order

core_rule: Use subjunctive after common triggers of necessity, wish, emotion, doubt, or possibility.
when_to_use: Use this when the main clause presents a subjective stance toward the following action.
contrast_rules: Subjunctive is triggered by the frame, not by English tense. | Certain or factual statements usually keep indicative.
main_traps: using indicative after il faut que | using subjunctive after every que | ignoring subject change
anchor_examples: Il faut que tu viennes. | Je veux qu'elle parte. | Je doute qu'il sache.
xray_sentence: Il faut que tu viennes demain.
xray_marks: Il faut que=>trigger=>necessity opens a subjunctive slot||viennes=>subjunctive_form=>venir changes form after the trigger

core_rule: Use en + present participle to express how, when, or while something happens.
when_to_use: Use this to compress two actions with the same subject into one sentence.
contrast_rules: Same-subject control matters; do not use the gerund if the subject changes. | It often expresses manner or simultaneity, not every English -ing.
main_traps: using it with a different subject | translating every -ing form | forgetting en
anchor_examples: Il apprend en écoutant. | Elle sourit en entrant. | En travaillant, tu progresseras.
xray_sentence: Elle a répondu en souriant.
xray_marks: en souriant=>gerund_phrase=>shows how she answered||Elle=>same_subject=>the same subject controls both actions

core_rule: Past participles agree with the subject with être, and with a preceding direct object in key avoir patterns.
when_to_use: Use this when writing passé composé carefully and the auxiliary or object order can affect agreement.
pattern: être + agreeing participle; preceding direct object + avoir agreement
contrast_rules: With avoir, agreement is not with the subject unless the direct object comes before. | Reflexive verbs require special attention.
main_traps: adding agreement after every avoir | missing être agreement | ignoring preceding direct objects
anchor_examples: Elle est arrivée. | Les lettres que j'ai écrites. | Ils se sont levés.
xray_sentence: Les lettres que j'ai écrites sont longues.
xray_marks: Les lettres=>preceding_object=>the direct object appears before the participle||écrites=>participle_agreement=>the participle agrees feminine plural

name_en: Subjunctive after will, emotion, and doubt
name_de: Subjonctif nach Wille, Gefühl und Zweifel
core_rule: Use subjunctive after frames that present an action as wanted, felt, doubted, necessary, or not yet factual.
when_to_use: Use this to express attitude toward an action rather than simply reporting it as fact.
contrast_rules: Indicative reports facts; subjunctive marks the speaker's stance. | Some expressions switch mood when negated or questioned.
main_traps: using indicative after doubt/wish | using subjunctive after certain facts | missing irregular subjunctive stems
anchor_examples: Je veux que tu viennes. | Je suis content qu'elle soit là. | Je doute qu'il puisse venir.
xray_sentence: Je doute qu'il puisse finir ce soir.
xray_marks: Je doute que=>doubt_trigger=>doubt opens a subjunctive frame||puisse=>subjunctive_form=>pouvoir takes subjunctive form

core_rule: The mood changes with how the speaker presents the clause: factual/certain uses indicative; subjective/uncertain uses subjunctive.
when_to_use: Use this when the same verb can appear after different frames with different meaning.
contrast_rules: Mood is not translated directly into English. | A small change in the main clause can change the mood.
main_traps: using one mood after every que | missing negative/question mood shifts | treating subjunctive as a tense
anchor_examples: Je pense qu'il vient. | Je ne pense pas qu'il vienne. | Il est clair qu'elle sait.
xray_sentence: Je pense qu'il vient, mais je doute qu'il vienne demain.
xray_marks: pense que=>certainty_frame=>pense que normally takes indicative||doute que=>doubt_frame=>doubt triggers subjunctive

core_rule: Use plus-que-parfait for a past action completed before another past reference point.
when_to_use: Use this to show “had done” relationships in narration, explanation, and reported memory.
contrast_rules: Plus-que-parfait is earlier-than-past; passé composé is the event point. | It often explains a later past result.
main_traps: using passé composé for both past layers | forgetting imparfait auxiliary | overusing it for simple chronology
anchor_examples: Quand je suis arrivé, elle était déjà partie. | J'avais oublié mes clés.
xray_sentence: Quand je suis arrivé, elle était déjà partie.
xray_marks: suis arrivé=>past_reference=>the arrival is the later past point||était partie=>earlier_past=>she had left before that point

core_rule: Use conditional past for a hypothetical or polite result viewed as completed.
when_to_use: Use this for regrets, unreal past results, and reported future-perfect ideas from a past viewpoint.
contrast_rules: Conditional past is not the same as plus-que-parfait; it marks dependency or unreal result. | Si type 3 uses plus-que-parfait in the si-clause.
main_traps: using it after si | using present conditional for completed results | forgetting être agreement
anchor_examples: J'aurais aimé venir. | Elle serait partie plus tôt. | Nous aurions pu aider.
xray_sentence: J'aurais répondu si j'avais vu ton message.
xray_marks: J'aurais répondu=>conditional_past_result=>the unreal completed result||si j'avais vu=>past_condition=>the condition uses plus-que-parfait

core_rule: Use future perfect for an action that will be completed before a future reference point.
when_to_use: Use this to say “will have done” or to mark completion before another future event.
contrast_rules: Future simple says what will happen; future perfect says what will already be completed. | It can also express probability about the past.
main_traps: using future simple for completed-before-future | forgetting auxiliary future form | missing être agreement
anchor_examples: Quand tu arriveras, j'aurai fini. | Elle sera déjà partie.
xray_sentence: Quand tu arriveras, j'aurai fini le rapport.
xray_marks: tu arriveras=>future_reference=>the arrival is the future point||j'aurai fini=>completed_before=>the report will be finished before that point

core_rule: Use plus-que-parfait after si and conditional past in the result for an unreal past condition.
when_to_use: Use this for regret, counterfactual explanation, and imagined alternatives to what happened.
contrast_rules: Si type 2 imagines present/future; si type 3 imagines a past that did not happen. | Do not put conditional after si.
main_traps: using conditional after si | using imparfait instead of plus-que-parfait | using present conditional for past result
anchor_examples: Si j'avais su, je serais venu. | Si elle avait étudié, elle aurait réussi.
xray_sentence: Si j'avais su, je serais venu plus tôt.
xray_marks: j'avais su=>past_condition=>plus-que-parfait marks the unreal past condition||serais venu=>conditional_past_result=>conditional past marks the unreal result

core_rule: Use passive voice when the receiver of the action is more important than the agent.
when_to_use: Use this in formal, news, and explanatory style when the actor is unknown, obvious, or secondary.
contrast_rules: Passive changes focus; active voice often sounds more direct. | The participle agrees with the passive subject.
main_traps: forgetting agreement | using avoir instead of être | overusing passive where active is clearer
anchor_examples: La décision a été annoncée. | Le livre est écrit par Camus.
xray_sentence: La loi a été votée par l'Assemblée.
xray_marks: La loi=>passive_subject=>the receiver becomes grammatical subject||a été votée=>passive_verb=>être + participle forms the passive and agrees

core_rule: Use faire + infinitive when someone causes or has someone else do an action.
when_to_use: Use this for “make/have/get something done” structures.
contrast_rules: The person causing the action is not necessarily performing it. | Pronoun placement can make the construction dense.
main_traps: conjugating the second verb | translating make literally | losing who does the action
anchor_examples: Je fais réparer la voiture. | Elle fait lire le texte aux élèves.
xray_sentence: Je fais réparer mon vélo demain.
xray_marks: fais=>causer=>the subject causes the repair||réparer=>infinitive_action=>the action someone else performs stays infinitive

core_rule: Use connectors such as bien que, quoique, pourtant, cependant, and en revanche to organize contrast.
when_to_use: Use this in argumentation when a sentence grants one point and then turns to another.
pattern: subjunctive after bien que/quoique; indicative after many adverbial connectors
contrast_rules: Some connectors trigger subjunctive; others only connect clauses. | Register matters: pourtant is common, néanmoins is formal.
main_traps: using indicative after bien que | overusing mais | choosing connector without register
anchor_examples: bien qu'il soit tard | pourtant, elle reste | en revanche, il refuse
xray_sentence: Bien qu'il soit tard, elle continue à travailler.
xray_marks: Bien que=>concession_trigger=>introduces a conceded fact and triggers subjunctive||soit=>subjunctive_form=>être appears in subjunctive after bien que

core_rule: Use lequel forms and preposition + relative pronouns when the relation is more precise than qui/que/dont.
when_to_use: Use this in formal or precise writing when a preposition links the noun to the relative clause.
contrast_rules: Lequel agrees with the antecedent. | Dont often replaces de + noun, but de + lequel appears after compound prepositions.
main_traps: using qui after a preposition for things | forgetting agreement | using dont after près de
anchor_examples: la raison pour laquelle | le projet auquel je pense | les outils avec lesquels je travaille
xray_sentence: C'est la raison pour laquelle je refuse.
xray_marks: la raison=>antecedent=>the feminine noun controls laquelle||pour laquelle=>preposition_relative=>the preposition remains attached to the relative form

core_rule: With compound tenses and infinitives, pronouns still occupy structured slots around the relevant verb.
when_to_use: Use this when two objects are replaced in sentences with passé composé, infinitives, or modal verbs.
pattern: pronoun order before auxiliary/conjugated verb; before infinitive when tied to infinitive
contrast_rules: Pronouns attach to the verb they complete semantically. | Agreement may depend on a preceding direct object.
main_traps: putting pronouns before the wrong verb | mixing order with imperatives | missing participle agreement
anchor_examples: Je le lui ai donné. | Je vais le lui dire. | Elle nous en a parlé.
xray_sentence: Je vais le lui envoyer demain.
xray_marks: vais=>conjugated_modal=>the future-near frame is conjugated here||le lui envoyer=>infinitive_pronouns=>the pronouns stay with the infinitive they complete

core_rule: Formal questions often use inversion, including complex inversion when the subject is a noun.
when_to_use: Use this in formal writing, interviews, and precise public-facing questions.
pattern: verb-subject pronoun inversion; noun subject + verb-pronoun echo
contrast_rules: Inversion is more formal than est-ce que. | Complex inversion repeats the noun subject with a pronoun.
main_traps: forgetting hyphen | omitting the echo pronoun | using spoken order in formal contexts
anchor_examples: Pourquoi vient-il ? | Marie viendra-t-elle ? | Où les étudiants vont-ils ?
xray_sentence: Le ministre répondra-t-il demain ?
xray_marks: Le ministre=>noun_subject=>the noun subject stays before the verb||répondra-t-il=>inversion_echo=>the pronoun echo creates formal inversion

core_rule: Ne explétif is a formal non-negative ne used after certain fear, prevention, comparison, or doubt expressions.
when_to_use: Use this mostly for recognition and polished formal writing; it does not make the clause negative.
pattern: trigger + ne explétif + verb without pas
contrast_rules: Ne explétif is not negation because there is no pas or other negative partner. | It is optional and register-sensitive.
main_traps: reading it as negative | adding pas | using it in casual speech
anchor_examples: Je crains qu'il ne vienne. | Avant qu'il ne parte.
xray_sentence: Je crains qu'il ne parte trop tôt.
xray_marks: Je crains que=>fear_trigger=>fear expressions can license ne explétif||ne parte=>non_negative_ne=>ne appears without pas and does not negate the verb

core_rule: Formal French uses negative pairs such as ne... point, ne... guère, ne... nullement, and ne... aucun.
when_to_use: Use this for reading advanced texts and for controlled formal style, not everyday conversation.
contrast_rules: These forms differ in intensity and register. | Ne... que is restrictive, not a normal negative pair.
main_traps: treating every ne as ne...pas | using literary forms in casual speech | confusing guère with jamais
anchor_examples: Il ne vient guère ici. | Elle n'a nullement accepté. | Nul ne sait.
xray_sentence: Il ne répond guère à ces critiques.
xray_marks: ne...guère=>formal_negative_pair=>guère means hardly/not much||à ces critiques=>scope=>the negative scope affects the response, not the existence of critiques

core_rule: Advanced tense sequence keeps each clause anchored to the time viewpoint created by reporting, narration, or hypothesis.
when_to_use: Use this when past reporting, anteriority, and modality interact across several clauses.
pattern: reporting viewpoint + embedded tense adjusted to relation in time
contrast_rules: Tense choice reflects relation to a viewpoint, not only calendar time. | Reported speech may preserve or shift tenses depending on meaning.
main_traps: flattening all clauses to one tense | losing anteriority | translating English backshift mechanically
anchor_examples: Il a expliqué qu'il avait déjà répondu. | Elle dira qu'elle aura terminé.
xray_sentence: Il a dit qu'il avait compris avant la réunion.
xray_marks: a dit=>past_viewpoint=>the reporting verb sets a past viewpoint||avait compris=>anterior_embedded=>plus-que-parfait marks understanding before that viewpoint

core_rule: Advanced passive choices decide whether the agent is expressed with par, de, or omitted entirely.
when_to_use: Use this in analytical writing when focus, responsibility, or stylistic distance matters.
pattern: passive être + participle + optional par/de agent
contrast_rules: Par often marks an action agent; de can mark state, feeling, or authorial relation. | Omission can hide or background responsibility.
main_traps: using par mechanically | overusing passive | missing agreement or agent nuance
anchor_examples: Le texte est écrit par Duras. | Il est connu de tous. | La mesure a été critiquée.
xray_sentence: La décision a été critiquée par plusieurs députés.
xray_marks: La décision=>focus_patient=>the decision is foregrounded||par plusieurs députés=>agent_phrase=>par names who performed the criticism

core_rule: Participial clauses condense a secondary action or state around the same subject or a clearly controlled noun.
when_to_use: Use this in written French to make dense, elegant sentences without losing who does what.
pattern: present/past participle phrase linked to controlled subject
contrast_rules: Participial clauses are compact but can become ambiguous if control is unclear. | They are more written than conversational.
main_traps: unclear subject control | overusing dense clauses | using a participle where a finite clause is needed
anchor_examples: Arrivant en retard, il s'est excusé. | Les documents signés seront envoyés.
xray_sentence: Arrivant en retard, elle s'est excusée.
xray_marks: Arrivant=>participial_action=>the participle compresses the first action||elle=>controlled_subject=>the subject of the main clause controls the participle

core_rule: Advanced connectors mark cause, concession, reformulation, consequence, and stance with precise register.
when_to_use: Use this in essays, summaries, and argumentation where the relation between ideas must be explicit.
contrast_rules: Connectors are not interchangeable translations of “but/so/because.” | Some connectors require subjunctive or a specific construction.
main_traps: choosing by English meaning only | mixing registers | ignoring mood after connector
anchor_examples: En effet, cela confirme l'hypothèse. | Cela dit, il faut nuancer. | Encore faut-il le prouver.
xray_sentence: Cela dit, il faut nuancer cette conclusion.
xray_marks: Cela dit=>concession_transition=>signals a turn after a previous point||nuancer=>argument_action=>the following verb states how the argument is adjusted
